content:
First, rephrase the forbidden‐adjacency condition in graph‐theoretic terms.  Think of the 1000×1000 grid as the vertex–edge graph G whose vertices are the unit‐squares and whose edges join orthogonally adjacent squares.  A domino covering two adjacent squares corresponds to choosing an edge of G.  The condition “no two dominoes are edge‐adjacent” means that if we pick two edges e,f of G to be dominoes, then it must not happen that an endpoint of e is adjacent (in G) to an endpoint of f.  Equivalently, the chosen edges form an induced matching in G.

A quick way to exhibit an induced matching of size 250 000 is to partition the board into 2×2 blocks:

–  Since 1000 is even, we can tile the entire board by 2×2 sub–boards in a 500×500 array of such blocks.  
–  In each 2×2 block, place exactly one domino (say, the top two squares of the block).  
–  No two of these dominoes can share an edge, because any two distinct 2×2 blocks meet only at most at a corner.  

This places exactly one domino in each of the 500·500=250 000 blocks, so we get 250 000 dominoes in all.

To see that you cannot do better, observe that each domino “occupies” its 2 squares and also forbids any other domino from using any of their edge‐neighbours.  In particular every 2×2 square of the board can contain at most one domino, so there cannot be more than one domino per 4‐cell block.  Since the whole board is 1 000 000 cells, you get the upper bound

\[
\hbox{max \#dominoes} \;\le\; \frac{1000\cdot1000}{4}\;=\;250\,000.
\]

Putting lower and upper bound together, the maximum possible is

\boxed{250000}
